summary: An excerpt from Sebastian Mallaby's book 'The Infinity Machine' detailing Demis Hassabis's early life as a chess prodigy. It highlights his extreme dedication, his identification with the sci-fi character Ender, and his pivotal realization that immense intellectual effort should be directed toward solving humanity's greatest scientific challenges rather than just winning games.

principles.statement: If you are tackling the universe's biggest problems, you have absolutely no time to waste.
principles.rationale: The sheer scale of understanding reality and solving humanity's problems requires total, relentless dedication without distraction.
principles.source_quote: If you are trying to solve humanity's problems and understand the nature of reality, you don't have any time to waste.

principles.statement: True effort means pushing yourself to the absolute limit of your endurance.
principles.rationale: You only know you have truly done your best if you have exhausted every ounce of your energy, stopping just short of destroying yourself.
principles.source_quote: The only way I could know is basically if I pushed myself to the point just before death. Because that is literally when you have done your best.

principles.statement: Exceptional mental effort should be harnessed for higher causes, not wasted on games.
principles.rationale: After witnessing brilliant minds exhaust themselves over chess, he realized that such immense collective brainpower should be applied to science or medicine.
principles.source_quote: I thought we were wasting our minds.

mental_models.name: The Ender's Game Persona
mental_models.description: Identifying with the sci-fi character Ender to frame one's life as a mission to save humanity or solve the universe, accepting the immense personal sacrifice, isolation, and extreme mental testing required to shoulder that responsibility.
mental_models.example: Hassabis works night shifts from 10 PM to 4 AM on top of normal hours, viewing his extreme endurance and lack of free time as necessary for the mission.

mental_models.name: The Marathon Finish Line
mental_models.description: A metaphor for evaluating whether you have truly given your best effort. If you cross the finish line and are still standing with energy left, you haven't tried hard enough.
mental_models.example: He applies this to his work ethic: "It's like running a marathon. You have to basically fall over the line. And then ideally you should be hospitalized, but not dead. That's when you can say you've done your best."

heuristics: If you've got any energy left, you're still standing, maybe you could have tried harder?

signature_quotes.text: If you are trying to solve humanity's problems and understand the nature of reality, you don't have any time to waste.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his grueling work schedule and identification with the character Ender.

signature_quotes.text: The only way I could know is basically if I pushed myself to the point just before death. Because that is literally when you have done your best.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on his childhood interpretation of his father's advice to 'try your best.'

signature_quotes.text: It's like running a marathon. You have to basically fall over the line. And then ideally you should be hospitalized, but not dead. That's when you can say you've done your best.
signature_quotes.context: Describing his extreme standard for personal effort.

signature_quotes.text: I thought we were wasting our minds.
signature_quotes.context: His epiphany after a grueling 10-hour chess match, realizing that brilliant brains should focus on science or medicine instead of board games.

anti_patterns.description: Wasting immense collective mental effort and stamina on zero-sum games.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It drains brilliant minds of their energy and potential, which could otherwise be harnessed to solve real-world problems in science and medicine.
